serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mutton seekh kebab is approximately 120g (2 skewers), providing around 362 kcal, 12.8g protein, 31.7g fat, and 6.3g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Seekh Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Seekh Kebab (cr_id G00010927). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on minced spiced meat preparation with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mutton seekh kebab. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous minced spiced meat preparations (e.g., kofta, kebab) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is low at 5.24g/100g, predominantly from spices and onion, which have minimal glycemic impact. GL per 120g serving = (30 × 5.24 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 1.9, rounded to 1.6 conservatively. This is a low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080mg/100g — far exceeds the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit mutton seekh kebab due to its very high sodium content of 1080mg/100g; if consumed, restrict to a single small skewer (≤60g) and avoid other high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24g/100g) and low GI (~30) are favourable, but high fat (26.4g/100g) and high caloric density (302 kcal/100g) require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton seekh kebab has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content, making it acceptable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes, but the high fat content (26.4g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance with frequent consumption; limit to one serving (120g) and pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than high-GI carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5g/100g), high sodium (1080mg/100g), and high cholesterol (85mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mutton seekh kebab poses a high cardiovascular concern due to its combination of elevated saturated fat (~11.5g/100g), very high sodium (1080mg/100g), and significant cholesterol (85mg/100g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this food only occasionally and in small portions, prioritising leaner protein alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton (lamb) is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), a primary pro-inflammatory trigger; saturated fat ~11.5g/100g exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid mutton seekh kebab as mutton is a red meat high in arachidonic acid that amplifies inflammatory pathways, and the high saturated fat and sodium content further worsen joint inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Seekh Kebab is a popular South Asian grilled meat dish made from minced lamb (mutton) blended with aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, garlic, green chili, and onion, then shaped onto metal or bamboo skewers and roasted or grilled over high heat until charred on the outside and juicy within. The dish originates from the Indian subcontinent and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore as a starter or main course at lunch and dinner. The texture is firm yet moist, with a smoky, spiced flavour profile. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 302 kcal, 10.7g protein, 26.4g fat (including approximately 11.5g saturated fat), 5.24g carbohydrate, 2.85g sugar, and 1080mg sodium. The high sodium content (1080mg/100g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. The low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index (~30) make it relatively acceptable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes, though the high fat content warrants moderation. The red meat base (mutton/lamb) is rich in arachidonic acid and is a pro-inflammatory food for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Calcium is notably present at 118mg/100g, likely from spice blends. This dish is typically halal and gluten-free.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010927) with a confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. Saturated fat estimated at ~11.5g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for minced lamb (ground lamb, raw) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is the only estimated field. All other values are exact from HPB.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 11.5g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for ground lamb (raw), USDA SR Legacy ID 17066, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat breakdown for this dish. This is a reasonable estimate given the fat content of 26.4g/100g for a minced lamb preparation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific mutton seekh kebab preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite dish as of 2026. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is a confirmed zero from the approved source for this minced meat preparation. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~30, low) as no direct GI measurement for mutton seekh kebab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; estimated from comparable minced spiced meat preparations. 5. Halal status assumed based on the Indian Muslim culinary tradition of seekh kebab; individual restaurant preparation should be verified. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 term exists for mutton seekh kebab specifically.
